title: What is a Product Engineer?
paragraph1: A Product Engineer helps you decide what to build and why, designs how the system should work so it fits your goals, and builds it themselves—or has the team build it. They don't just take tickets: they help shape the plan, the technical choices, and the delivery. One person who can take you from "we're not sure what we need" or "our tech is a mess" to a clear plan and a product that works. In 2026 AI is everywhere, and that boosts this profile: the one who decides where AI fits in the system, where the guardrails are, and what actually goes out the door—instead of just prompting.
paragraph2: In practice: one person who can talk product with you, make the technical decisions, and get the work delivered. No handoff, no black box. With AI, needs and scope can be identified in conversation and carried through to development—the wall between "what we want" and "what we build" shrinks, and productivity goes up. Not an "AI operator": the person who steers how the product and the system use AI. In 2026 that’s the profile that wins.

rows.developer: Features and bugs, within a given scope.
rows.productManager: What to build, for whom, in what order.
rows.productEngineer: What to build and how; product and tech in one role.
Outcomes, architecture, delivery; the how and part of the what.

rows.techLead: Broad and deep; guides stack and design.
rows.productManager: Often light; relies on eng for how.

rows.productEngineer: Tech choices tied to product and business.
Owns or co-owns structure, boundaries, debt.
Ships and shapes what and how.
